meta.title: Signal & Noise: A Radio Telescope Data Pipeline
meta.learner_goal_summary: The learner wants to build a working multi-stage data processing pipeline using Python, gaining practical fluency with generators, iterators, and functional tools like map, filter, and reduce by constructing something that feels like real engineering work.
primary_concepts: map and filter as pipeline stages
secondary_concepts: separation of data production from data consumption
assumptions_made: No explicit theme was specified by the learner, so a radio telescope signal-processing theme was chosen because it has a natural and clean analogy: raw signals arrive as a potentially infinite stream, each stage of the pipeline filters or transforms without loading everything into memory, and the final stage aggregates into a result — mirroring generators, map/filter, and reduce precisely.
Time budget was not specified precisely, so 140 minutes was chosen to allow four meaty parts at roughly 30-35 minutes each with a short capstone integration part.
The learner is assumed comfortable with basic Python (functions, loops, lists, basic classes) but has not deliberately used generators or functional tools before.
No visualization requirement was stated; a text-based printout of pipeline results is used as the payoff to keep scope manageable.
Standard library only (itertools, functools) — no third-party dependencies assumed.

narrative_brief.theme: Deep-space radio telescope signal processing station
narrative_brief.setting: The Peregrine Array, a remote automated radio telescope outpost that receives a continuous stream of raw signal readings from deep space. The learner is the lone on-duty Signal Analyst keeping the data pipeline running overnight.
narrative_brief.premise: The Peregrine Array never stops receiving data — signals pour in faster than any list can hold them. The station's ancient batch-processing software has failed, and the learner must rebuild the pipeline stage by stage: first generating the raw signal stream, then cleaning and transforming it, then filtering out noise, and finally aggregating the results into nightly reports. Each stage must hand data to the next without ever loading the whole stream into memory at once.
narrative_brief.tone: Quiet, atmospheric, and technically precise — like working a night shift at a remote outpost with only your instruments and a thermos of coffee. Deadpan dry humor is welcome; no melodrama. The 'stakes' are gently whimsical (the data just gets logged, nobody dies) but the engineering problem is real.
recurring_motifs: the signal stream as an endless river of numbers
'noise floor' as the threshold below which readings are discarded
pipeline stages as physical filters bolted in sequence on a rack
ticks or 'sample windows' as the unit of time
the nightly report as the final aggregated output filed before dawn
narrative_brief.protagonist_or_frame_device: The learner is the Signal Analyst on the overnight shift, writing the pipeline code that processes the Peregrine Array's incoming data before the morning crew arrives.
narrative_brief.opening_hook_guidance: Open with the Signal Analyst arriving at the console to find the old pipeline software crashed and a blinking cursor waiting. The raw signal feed is live and accumulating. Keep the hook under 120 words and end with a concrete first task: 'You need a generator that can produce signal samples on demand — because reading them all into a list at once would crash the station's memory in under a minute.'
narrative_brief.closing_note_guidance: Close by having the learner's own pipeline print the nightly report — a few lines of in-world output showing how many samples were processed, the strongest signal detected, and the average signal strength above the noise floor. Frame it as the log entry the morning crew will find. Should feel like a small, satisfying payoff, not a lecture recap.

learning_objectives.objective: Write a generator function using yield to produce values lazily on demand
Explain why a generator uses less memory than a list for large or infinite sequences
Use map() with a transformation function to produce a new lazy stream from an existing iterator
Use filter() with a predicate function to selectively pass values through a pipeline stage
Use functools.reduce() to collapse a finite iterable into a single aggregated value
Compose multiple generator and functional-tool stages into a single end-to-end lazy pipeline

assumed_knowledge: Python functions with parameters and return values
explicit_non_goals: asyncio or async generators — this set is entirely synchronous
itertools beyond islice (which is provided as scaffolding) — do not introduce tee, chain, groupby, etc.
class-based iterators with __iter__ and __next__ — yield-based generators only
file I/O or real signal data formats

files.purpose: Main skeleton file the learner edits directly; contains all stubs and provided helpers
Provided run script that imports from peregrine.py and calls run_pipeline() to print the nightly report; the learner does not edit this file

provided_classes_or_functions.name: take
provided_classes_or_functions.role: Wraps itertools.islice — consumes exactly n items from any iterator and returns them as a list. Provided so the learner can materialize a finite slice of an infinite generator for testing without needing to know islice syntax yet.
provided_classes_or_functions.notes_for_code_agent: Implement as: from itertools import islice; def take(n, iterable): return list(islice(iterable, n)). Include a one-line docstring. Keep it at the top of the file as a utility the learner will use in every part.

provided_classes_or_functions.name: NOISE_FLOOR
provided_classes_or_functions.role: Module-level constant float set to 0.25. Represents the minimum signal strength a reading must exceed to be considered real rather than background noise. Used as the threshold in part_3's filter stage.
provided_classes_or_functions.notes_for_code_agent: Define as NOISE_FLOOR = 0.25 near the top of the file, below imports, above all stubs. The Story Agent should frame this as a calibrated instrument constant that the analyst is not supposed to change.

provided_classes_or_functions.name: CALIBRATION_OFFSET
provided_classes_or_functions.role: Module-level constant float set to 0.05. Represents a known systematic bias in the antenna hardware that must be subtracted from every raw reading during calibration. Used in part_2's map stage.
provided_classes_or_functions.notes_for_code_agent: Define as CALIBRATION_OFFSET = 0.05 near the top of the file alongside NOISE_FLOOR.

environment_notes: Standard CPython 3.8+ environment; no third-party packages required.
All randomness in tests should be controlled via random.seed() in the test harness.
peregrine_runner.py should import peregrine and call peregrine.run_pipeline(num_samples=500, seed=42) so results are reproducible when demonstrated.

parts.title: The Signal Feed
parts.narrative_tie_in: The raw antenna feed produces a continuous stream of floating-point signal strength readings between 0.0 and 1.0. The old software tried to collect them all into a list first — that's why it crashed. The analyst's first job is to write a generator that produces readings one at a time, on demand, forever.
parts.learner_task_spec: Implement a generator function signal_feed(seed=None) that, when called, yields an infinite sequence of simulated raw signal readings. Each reading is a float drawn from random.uniform(0.0, 1.0). If seed is not None, the generator should call random.seed(seed) exactly once before beginning to yield values. The generator must never return — it yields indefinitely. The learner should also implement a second generator bounded_signal_feed(n, seed=None) that yields exactly n readings and then stops, to make manual testing easier. Both must be true generators (using yield), not functions that return lists.

entities.name: signal_feed
entities.docstring_requirements: Describe that this is an infinite generator of float readings in [0.0, 1.0], that seed controls reproducibility, and that the caller must use take() or itertools.islice to consume a finite slice.

entities.name: bounded_signal_feed
entities.docstring_requirements: Describe that this yields exactly n readings then stops; useful for testing without islice.

edge_cases_to_handle: take(0, signal_feed()) should return an empty list without hanging
calling signal_feed() twice without a seed should produce independent (non-identical) streams
calling signal_feed(seed=42) twice should produce identical streams (reproducibility test)
bounded_signal_feed(n) should yield exactly n items — check with len(list(bounded_signal_feed(7))) == 7

hints_tiered.tier_1_nudge: A generator function looks just like a regular function but uses 'yield' instead of 'return'. What does that change about when the code inside runs?
hints_tiered.tier_2_partial: An infinite generator is just a while True loop with a yield inside. Python will pause the function at each yield and resume it the next time the caller asks for a value.
hints_tiered.tier_3_near_solution: signal_feed: optionally call random.seed(seed), then while True: yield random.uniform(0.0, 1.0). For bounded_signal_feed: use a for loop over range(n) with yield inside instead of while True.

sample_test_cases.description: take(5, signal_feed()) returns a list of exactly 5 floats
sample_test_cases.expected_behavior: result is a list of length 5, all values in [0.0, 1.0], type float

sample_test_cases.description: seeded feed is reproducible
signal_feed is a generator object, not a list
bounded_signal_feed yields exactly n items
bounded_signal_feed(0) yields nothing

grading_rubric.criterion: signal_feed uses yield and is a true generator (not returning a list)
seeded reproducibility works correctly
bounded_signal_feed yields exactly n values and stops
all yielded values are floats in [0.0, 1.0]

common_mistakes_to_flag: using return inside a generator body with a list instead of yield — produces a TypeError when iterated
calling random.seed() on every iteration instead of once at the start, which breaks the intended distribution
forgetting to handle seed=None (should not call random.seed at all in that case, or behavior will be deterministic unintentionally)

parts.title: The Calibration Stage
parts.narrative_tie_in: Every reading coming off the antenna has a known hardware bias baked in — the instrument consistently reads 0.05 too high due to thermal drift in the receiver. Before any analysis can happen, each raw reading must have CALIBRATION_OFFSET subtracted. The analyst writes a calibration stage that transforms the raw feed without touching the generator itself.
parts.learner_task_spec: Implement a function calibrate(stream) that takes any iterator of raw float readings and returns a new lazy iterator where each value has CALIBRATION_OFFSET subtracted. The returned object must be lazy — it must not materialize the stream into a list internally. Implement it using map() with a lambda or a named helper function. Then implement a second function normalize(stream) that takes any iterator of floats and returns a lazy iterator where each value is clamped to [0.0, 1.0] using min(1.0, max(0.0, value)) — because after calibration, some values might go negative or above 1.0. Both calibrate and normalize must be composable: normalize(calibrate(signal_feed())) must work as a single lazy chain.

entities.name: calibrate
entities.docstring_requirements: State that stream is any iterator of floats, that CALIBRATION_OFFSET is subtracted from each value, and that the returned object is lazy (a map object or generator).

entities.name: normalize
entities.docstring_requirements: State that stream is any iterator of floats, that each value is clamped to [0.0, 1.0], and that the returned object is lazy.

edge_cases_to_handle: a raw reading of exactly 0.0 after calibration becomes -0.05 — normalize must clamp it to 0.0
a raw reading of 1.0 after calibration becomes 0.95 — should remain 0.95, not be clamped
calibrate applied to an empty iterator should return an empty iterator without error
the returned objects must support being iterated with a for loop or passed to take()

hints_tiered.tier_1_nudge: map() takes a function and an iterable and returns a lazy object that applies the function to each element one at a time. How do you write a function that subtracts a fixed number from its input?
hints_tiered.tier_2_partial: calibrate can be a single line: return map(lambda x: x - CALIBRATION_OFFSET, stream). The returned map object is lazy — it won't compute anything until iterated.
hints_tiered.tier_3_near_solution: normalize: return map(lambda x: min(1.0, max(0.0, x)), stream). To chain them: normalize(calibrate(stream)) — each map wraps the previous iterator without materializing it.

sample_test_cases.description: calibrate subtracts CALIBRATION_OFFSET from every value
normalize clamps negative values to 0.0
calibrate returns a lazy object, not a list
sample_test_cases.expected_behavior: not a list — has __iter__ and __next__ (is a map object or generator)

sample_test_cases.description: chained pipeline calibrate -> normalize stays lazy

grading_rubric.criterion: calibrate correctly subtracts CALIBRATION_OFFSET using map()
normalize correctly clamps values using map()
both return lazy objects (not lists)
chaining works: normalize(calibrate(signal_feed())) is iterable

common_mistakes_to_flag: wrapping the result in list() inside the function body, destroying laziness
using a list comprehension instead of map(), which also materializes the stream
forgetting that CALIBRATION_OFFSET is a module-level constant and redeclaring it locally

parts.title: The Noise Gate
parts.narrative_tie_in: Most readings coming off a radio telescope are just background noise — thermal static, interference from satellites, cosmic microwave background. The noise gate rejects any calibrated reading that falls below NOISE_FLOOR, letting only meaningful signal detections pass through to analysis. The analyst adds a filter stage to the pipeline.
parts.learner_task_spec: Implement a function above_noise_floor(stream) that takes any iterator of calibrated float readings and returns a new lazy iterator containing only those values that are strictly greater than NOISE_FLOOR. Must be implemented using filter() with a lambda or named predicate function — not a generator loop or list comprehension. Then implement a second function strong_signal(stream, threshold) that filters to values strictly greater than an arbitrary threshold passed as a parameter, making the filter stage reusable for different sensitivity settings. Both must be lazy.

entities.name: above_noise_floor
entities.docstring_requirements: State that it uses NOISE_FLOOR as the cutoff, that equality does not pass (strictly greater than), and that the result is lazy.

entities.name: strong_signal
entities.docstring_requirements: State that threshold is a float parameter, that it filters to values strictly greater than threshold, and that the result is lazy.

edge_cases_to_handle: a reading equal to NOISE_FLOOR exactly (0.25) must NOT pass through — strictly greater than
a stream where every value is below the noise floor should yield an empty iterator without error
strong_signal with threshold=0.0 should pass all non-negative values
strong_signal with threshold=1.0 should pass no values from a normalized stream

hints_tiered.tier_1_nudge: filter() takes a predicate function (returns True or False) and an iterable. It yields only the values for which the predicate returns True. How do you express 'strictly greater than NOISE_FLOOR' as a function?
hints_tiered.tier_2_partial: above_noise_floor: return filter(lambda x: x > NOISE_FLOOR, stream). The lambda returns True only for values that should pass through.
hints_tiered.tier_3_near_solution: strong_signal: return filter(lambda x: x > threshold, stream). Note that threshold must be captured in the lambda's closure — it's a parameter of the outer function, so Python captures it automatically.

sample_test_cases.description: values at exactly NOISE_FLOOR are rejected
all-below-threshold stream returns empty
strong_signal with custom threshold
above_noise_floor is lazy — returns a filter object
full chain: signal_feed -> calibrate -> normalize -> above_noise_floor is iterable
sample_test_cases.expected_behavior: list of up to 10 floats, all strictly greater than 0.25

grading_rubric.criterion: above_noise_floor correctly uses NOISE_FLOOR as strict threshold via filter()
strong_signal correctly parameterizes the threshold
both return lazy objects
strict inequality (not >=) is correctly enforced

common_mistakes_to_flag: using >= instead of > for the threshold check, letting noise-floor-equal values pass
implementing as a generator loop or list comprehension instead of filter()
capturing threshold incorrectly in the lambda if restructured into a nested function

parts.title: The Nightly Aggregation
concept_focus: when and why to use reduce vs. built-ins
parts.narrative_tie_in: Once the filtered signal stream is gated, the analyst must collapse a finite window of readings into summary statistics for the nightly report: the total accumulated signal energy (sum), the peak reading (maximum), and a running product representing a joint probability estimate. Each of these must be computed using reduce(), so the analyst sees directly how a single binary operation applied repeatedly can produce different aggregations.
parts.learner_task_spec: Implement three aggregation functions, each taking a finite list of floats (already materialized from the pipeline) and returning a single value, each using functools.reduce() as the core computation mechanism. (1) total_energy(readings) — returns the sum of all values using reduce() with an addition lambda; must return 0.0 for an empty list using reduce's initializer parameter. (2) peak_reading(readings) — returns the maximum value using reduce() with a max-of-two lambda; must raise ValueError with message 'No readings above noise floor' for an empty list (do not use the initializer parameter here — instead check length before calling reduce). (3) joint_probability(readings) — returns the product of all values using reduce() with a multiplication lambda; must return 1.0 for an empty list using reduce's initializer parameter. All three must use reduce() explicitly — not sum(), max(), or math.prod().

entities.name: total_energy
entities.docstring_requirements: State that it sums via reduce(), returns 0.0 for empty input, and does not use the built-in sum().

entities.name: peak_reading
entities.docstring_requirements: State that it finds the maximum via reduce(), raises ValueError with message 'No readings above noise floor' for empty input, and does not use the built-in max().

entities.name: joint_probability
entities.docstring_requirements: State that it multiplies all values via reduce(), returns 1.0 for empty input, and does not use math.prod().

edge_cases_to_handle: empty list to total_energy must return 0.0 (not crash) — use the initializer argument of reduce()
empty list to peak_reading must raise ValueError with the exact message 'No readings above noise floor'
empty list to joint_probability must return 1.0 (not crash) — use the initializer argument of reduce()
single-element list to each function should return that element (or 1.0 * element for joint_probability)
joint_probability with values all less than 1.0 will produce a very small float — this is expected, not a bug

hints_tiered.tier_1_nudge: reduce(f, [a, b, c, d]) computes f(f(f(a, b), c), d) — it collapses a list using a two-argument function repeatedly. What two-argument function produces a sum? A maximum? A product?
hints_tiered.tier_2_partial: reduce(lambda a, b: a + b, readings, 0.0) sums the list and returns 0.0 if it's empty — the third argument to reduce() is the initial value used when the list is empty. For peak_reading, do NOT provide an initializer; instead guard with 'if not readings: raise ValueError(...)' before calling reduce.
hints_tiered.tier_3_near_solution: total_energy: return reduce(lambda a, b: a + b, readings, 0.0). peak_reading: if not readings: raise ValueError('No readings above noise floor'); return reduce(lambda a, b: a if a > b else b, readings). joint_probability: return reduce(lambda a, b: a * b, readings, 1.0).

sample_test_cases.description: total_energy sums correctly
total_energy on empty list returns 0.0
peak_reading returns maximum
peak_reading on empty list raises ValueError with correct message
sample_test_cases.expected_behavior: raises ValueError, message is exactly 'No readings above noise floor'

sample_test_cases.description: joint_probability on empty list returns 1.0
joint_probability on single element returns that element

grading_rubric.criterion: all three functions use reduce() explicitly (not sum/max/prod)
correct initializer usage in total_energy and joint_probability for empty-list case
peak_reading raises ValueError with exact message for empty list
all three return correct values on non-empty lists

common_mistakes_to_flag: using sum() or max() instead of reduce() — should be flagged by autograder checking for those built-in names in the function body via AST inspection if possible
providing an initializer of 0.0 to peak_reading — this makes max over floats return 0.0 on an empty list instead of raising, silently breaking the contract
using -math.inf as a manual initial value for peak_reading instead of the specified ValueError guard — acceptable but must still be flagged as 'not what the spec asked for'

parts.title: The Overnight Run
parts.narrative_tie_in: It's finally time to run the full pipeline before dawn. The Signal Analyst wires together every stage built so far into a single function that processes a configurable number of samples end-to-end and returns the nightly report as a dictionary. This is the morning log entry.
parts.learner_task_spec: Implement run_pipeline(num_samples, seed=None) that: (1) creates an infinite signal_feed with the given seed; (2) passes it through calibrate(); (3) passes the result through normalize(); (4) passes the result through above_noise_floor(); (5) uses take() to materialize exactly num_samples values from the filtered stream into a list called clean_readings; (6) computes and returns a dictionary with keys 'samples_collected' (int — length of clean_readings), 'total_energy' (float — from total_energy()), 'peak_reading' (float — from peak_reading(), or None if clean_readings is empty), and 'joint_probability' (float — from joint_probability()). The pipeline chain in steps 1-4 must remain lazy until take() is called in step 5. The function must handle the case where clean_readings is empty (peak_reading raises — catch it and store None).

entities.name: run_pipeline
entities.docstring_requirements: Full parameter and return contract: describe each pipeline stage in order, describe the returned dictionary keys and their types, and note the empty-stream handling.

edge_cases_to_handle: if the noise gate rejects so many readings that take() has consumed a very large number of raw samples — this is expected behavior, not an infinite loop bug, as long as signal_feed is truly infinite
num_samples=0 should return a dictionary with samples_collected=0, total_energy=0.0, peak_reading=None, joint_probability=1.0
the pipeline must not store all raw samples in memory — only clean_readings (post-filter) should be materialized
seed must propagate correctly so results are reproducible

hints_tiered.tier_1_nudge: Think of this function as physically bolting together the rack of filters you built in earlier parts. Each stage wraps the previous one. Where exactly in the chain should take() be called, and why not earlier?
hints_tiered.tier_2_partial: raw = signal_feed(seed=seed); cal = calibrate(raw); norm = normalize(cal); gated = above_noise_floor(norm); clean_readings = take(num_samples, gated). At this point clean_readings is a regular Python list and the lazy chain is exhausted for those n items.
hints_tiered.tier_3_near_solution: For the peak_reading key: try/except the ValueError from peak_reading(clean_readings) and store None in the except branch. Everything else is direct calls to the part_4 functions with clean_readings as input.

sample_test_cases.description: run_pipeline returns a dict with all four required keys
sample_test_cases.expected_behavior: returned value is a dict with keys 'samples_collected', 'total_energy', 'peak_reading', 'joint_probability'

sample_test_cases.description: samples_collected equals num_samples when stream is rich enough
peak_reading is None when num_samples=0
total_energy is 0.0 when num_samples=0
all clean_readings values in the pipeline are above NOISE_FLOOR
sample_test_cases.input: internal check: instrument run_pipeline to inspect clean_readings

sample_test_cases.description: seeded results are reproducible

grading_rubric.criterion: pipeline stages composed in the correct order (feed -> calibrate -> normalize -> filter -> take)
lazy chain not materialized before take()
correct dictionary structure with all four keys
empty stream handled correctly — peak_reading is None, others have correct neutral values
seeded reproducibility works end-to-end

common_mistakes_to_flag: calling take() or list() on the stream before the filter stage, materializing noise readings into memory
catching the wrong exception type (e.g. bare Exception instead of ValueError) in the peak_reading guard
forgetting to pass seed through to signal_feed, making results non-reproducible

global_verification_strategy.autograder_approach: Parts 1-4 are fully unit-testable with fixed random seeds. Each test module seeds random.seed(42) in a setUp/fixture before each test. Part 5 is an integration test that checks all four dictionary keys and validates that the pipeline produces values consistent with the stage invariants established in parts 1-4. An optional AST-level check for part 4 can scan for use of sum(), max(), or math.prod() in the function bodies and emit a warning if found, flagging possible bypass of the reduce() requirement.
global_verification_strategy.test_harness_notes: Tests for each part should be runnable independently so learners can check their work part by part. A shared conftest.py (or equivalent test setup) should import peregrine and seed random before each test. Part 5 integration tests should import run_pipeline and verify against a reference output computed offline with seed=42 and num_samples=200. The test for laziness (checking that functions return non-list iterables) should use hasattr(result, '__next__') rather than isinstance checks to stay robust across map objects and generator objects.
manual_review_needed_for: Part 2 and 3: confirming calibrate and normalize use map() and filter() rather than generator expressions — a runtime laziness test catches materialization but not the use of list comprehensions that happen to be written as generator expressions; an AST scan or brief manual read is more reliable
Part 4: confirming reduce() is used and not bypassed with sum()/max()/prod() equivalents — AST scan is preferred but manual review is a fallback
Part 5: confirming the lazy chain order is correct by code inspection, since a test can only observe outputs, not internal wiring

optional_extensions.title: The Interference Window
optional_extensions.description: Add a new pipeline stage interference_filter(stream, window_size) that uses a generator with yield to compute a sliding window average over the stream and yields only values whose raw value exceeds the window average by at least 0.1, detecting 'spikes' above local baseline. Requires the learner to maintain internal state inside a generator, introducing stateful generators as a natural extension of part 1.

optional_extensions.title: The Dual-Band Comparison
optional_extensions.description: Run two parallel pipelines with different NOISE_FLOOR thresholds (using strong_signal() from part 3 instead of above_noise_floor()) and use zip() to pair up their outputs sample-by-sample, then compute the correlation between the two filtered streams using reduce(). Reinforces polymorphic pipeline composition and introduces zip() as a lazy combinator.

optional_extensions.title: Generator Expressions vs. map/filter
optional_extensions.description: Rewrite calibrate, normalize, and above_noise_floor using generator expressions instead of map()/filter(), then use the take() function to confirm the outputs are identical. Write a brief in-code comment explaining when you would prefer one form over the other. This is a reflection/analysis task that deepens understanding of the relationship between the two styles.

style_guide_for_downstream_agents.tone: Quiet, atmospheric night-shift engineering — precise and unembellished, with dry wit allowed sparingly. Never use exclamation points in problem descriptions. Frame stakes as professional interest, not urgency or peril. The analyst is competent and calm, not panicked.
style_guide_for_downstream_agents.code_style: PEP 8 throughout. snake_case for all functions and variables. UPPER_SNAKE_CASE for module-level constants. No type hints in starter code (to avoid adding cognitive load not in scope). Docstrings on every public function using the single-paragraph style (no Google/NumPy sectioned style). Lambdas should be kept to one expression; if a lambda would exceed ~40 characters, use a named inner function instead.
consistency_rules: Function names signal_feed, bounded_signal_feed, calibrate, normalize, above_noise_floor, strong_signal, total_energy, peak_reading, joint_probability, and run_pipeline must be used verbatim by every downstream agent across descriptions, code, and tests — do not rename, alias, or retheme them.
Constants NOISE_FLOOR (0.25) and CALIBRATION_OFFSET (0.05) must appear at module level in peregrine.py and must not be redefined locally in any downstream agent's code or tests.

The helper function take(n, iterable) must be provided and used consistently — downstream agents must not substitute islice directly in learner-facing code examples, as take() is the learner's interface to consuming lazy streams throughout the set.

The word 'lazy' (not 'deferred', 'on-demand', or 'streaming') is the preferred learner-facing vocabulary for describing generator and map/filter behavior — use it consistently across all description and hint text.
Error message for peak_reading on empty input must be exactly the string 'No readings above noise floor' — tests check this string literally.
